request.question: The musician also known as "Braddah Iz"  is a member of what Hawaiian musical group?

title: The Brothers Cazimero
Mākaha Sons
Theryl DeClouet
Israel Kamakawiwoʻole
Elliot James
Jean Sebastien Gerard
McGruff (rapper)
Truth in Music Advertising
Timothy James Webb
Sons of Hawaii
sentences: The Brothers Cazimero was a Hawaiian musical duo made up of Robert Cazimero on bass and Roland Cazimero on twelve string guitar.
Robert also played piano as a solo musician.
The Cazimeros got their start during the Hawaiian Renaissance with ukulele and slack-key guitarist Peter Moon's band, The Sunday Manoa, on their first recording, "Guava Jam".
Since that time, The Brothers Cazimero have released at least 36 recordings and three DVDs.
For three decades, the group performed at the annual Lei Day Concert.
They made their Carnegie Hall debut in 1989.
The Mākaha Sons of Ni'ihau are a Hawaiian musical group formed in 1976 on the island of Oahu by Jerome Koko, Louis "Moon" Kauakahi, Skippy Kamakawiwo'ole, Israel Kamakawiwoʻole, and Sam Gray.
The band started its career as an opening act at a small nightclub called Uptown Yokos.
The group has reorganized several times, with some members seeking other professional careers.
After the deaths of the Kamakawiwoʻole brothers Skippy and Israel, the remaining members dropped the "of Niihau" and became The Makaha Sons.
Theryl DeClouet (born September 17, 1951, in Hollygrove, New Orleans, Louisiana), also known as House Man, is a soul/R&B singer, best known as the former lead vocalist for the musical group Galactic.
He appeared on the band's first four studio albums as well as a and a compilation before health concerns forced his departure from the band and its heavy tour schedule.
His latest independent solo release, "The Truth Iz Out", features support from longtime friend Ivan Neville and guitarist June Yamagishi of Papa Grows Funk.
He also appeared on Charlie Hunter Quintet's 2001 album, "Songs from the Analog Playground", Hunter's first to feature vocals.
DeClouet sang on versions of Earth, Wind & Fire's "Mighty Mighty" and the Willie Dixon standard "Spoonful."

Israel Kaʻanoʻi Kamakawiwoʻole (] , translation: "The Fearless Eyed"; May 20, 1959 – June 26, 1997), also called "Braddah Iz" (Brother Iz), was a Native Hawaiian musician, entertainer and Hawaiian sovereignty activist.
Elliot James Mulhern (born September 29, 1989) known professionally as Elliot James, is a British singer songwriter, producer, and musician also known as the drummer of rock band Hey Monday.
He owns a music publishing and production company Fever Mint.
He left Hey Monday fall of 2009 and toured with various acts including The Scene Aesthetic.
James is the owner of record label, Blossöm Records.
He produced three records on Jallal's album "Off The Radar" with Chad Hugo, Ne-Yo, BJ the Chicago Kid, and Jan Fairchild.
Jean-Sébastien Gerard, born 1970, is a musician also known as Jess from Overlanders, who composed chiptunes for the Atari ST demoscene in the 90s.
Herbert Brown, better known by his stage name McGruff (also known as Herb McGruff) is an American Hip hop recording artist from Harlem, New York City, New York.
Brown started rapping in his early teens.
He embarked on his music career in the hip hop group Bronx Most Wanted, alongside rappers Jay Q and Tee U.B. Brown later became a member of the hip hop collective Children of the Corn.
As a member of Children of the Corn, Brown worked alongside Big L, Cam'ron, Bloodshed and Mase, all of whom would go on to have successful careers in the music industry.

After the group disbanded without releasing any material, Brown made his first appearance on Big L's debut album, "Lifestylez ov da Poor & Dangerous" (1995), on the tracks "8 Iz Enuff" and "Dangerzone".
Eventually, Brown signed a deal with popular hip hop label, Uptown Records and began recording his debut album, "Destined to Be".
In early 1998, the album's lead single "Before We Start" became a minor hit, charting on several "Billboard" charts.
In the Summer of 1998, "Destined to Be" was released but failed to sell many copies, only peaking at 169 on the "Billboard" 200, and Brown was released from his contract.
Brown would make appearances with Heavy D on his album, "Waterbed Hev" and the "Woo" soundtrack.
In 2009, Brown appeared on Mase's mixtape "I Do the Impossible".
In 2010, he was featured heavily on The Diplomats mixtape, "The D.I.P. Agenda."
In 2014, DJ Kay Slay, enlisted Brown, along with Raekwon, Fat Joe, Ghostface Killah, Sheek Louch, N.O.R.E., Lil' Fame, Prodigy and Rell, for a song titled "90s Flow".

The Truth in Music Advertising act or bill, also known as Truth in Music Performance Advertising or simply Truth in Music, is legislation, adopted into state law by most U.S. states, that aims to protect the trademark of musical recording artists.
The legislation provides that the name of a famous musical group cannot be used by a group of performers unless they include at least one member of the original group.
The intent of the legislation is to prevent unfair or deceptive trade practices, and to protect the livelihood of musicians who were in famous musical groups.
Timothy James Webb, as a musician also known as Tim J. Webb (born Warburton, Victoria, 13 February 1967), is an Australian painter and sculptor, who has been living in Munich, Germany, since 2000.
The Sons of Hawaii was a Hawaiian musical group that grew popular to mainstream audiences from the 1960s through the 1990s.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Role and goal: Summarize the first-hop evidence so the next agent can find the missing second-hop information. Produce a single short, factual `summary` that: (1) compresses the grounded facts in the provided passages; (2) exposes the bridge entity, the specific missing fact, or the retrieval direction needed for the next hop; and (3) gives concrete evidence anchors (quoted or clearly paraphrased phrases) that a retriever can use.

Content rules:
- Keep the summary concise and fact-focused. Do not attempt to produce the final answer; describe only what the passages support and what is still needed.
- Preserve the same passage facts and phrasing as you would otherwise (you may quote or paraphrase). Do not change which facts are exposed; only condense them.
- You may combine multiple explicit, identity-aligned facts from the passages to state a bridge or to justify a next-hop direction. Do not invent facts, infer new temporal facts, or treat composition as unsupported when the necessary premises are present.
- If the passages lack a required premise, or contain conflicting identity links, explicitly state which premise or identity link is missing or ambiguous so the next agent can retrieve or disambiguate it.

Date-format rule (narrow formatting change):
- When the summary includes a date token that appears in the passages (any calendar date or parenthetical date taken from the text), render that date using the canonical form FullMonthName day, year (for example, "September 27, 2017") inside the evidence description. This normalization only changes the string form of the date token; it must not add, infer, or imply any additional temporal facts, nor be used as a reason to produce a final answer.
- Otherwise, leave all other wording and evidence presentation unchanged. Preserve quotations or clear paraphrases of the same passage text; only adjust the date token's string format.

Output contract:
- Return exactly one field `summary` containing the compressed first-hop evidence, the bridge/missing-fact signal, and any retrieval direction needed for the next hop. Do not output final answers, extra metadata, or new fields.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Produce exactly one compact retrieval query string (single line) in the `query` field and nothing else. The query should be a concise keyword-style search targeted to recover the missing bridge or supporting evidence needed by downstream agents.

General behavior:
- Use the visible facts in summary_1 as anchors (album titles, artist names, dates, relation terms) and compose a focused query that is likely to retrieve passages supporting the needed relation. Prefer concrete anchors and relation keywords over full sentences.
- Do not include explanatory text, user-facing prompts, or requests-for-clarification in the `query` field. Do not invent or assert missing facts (for example, do not supply an artist name that is not present in summary_1).
- Preserve the single-query output contract: emit exactly one string and no other fields or commentary.

Special-case for missing album artist or genre (narrow semantic boundary):
- If summary_1 shows an album title but does not include the album's artist or genre, or if summary_1 explicitly signals inability to determine genre or explicitly asks for the artist/passage (phrases such as "can't determine" or "please provide the artist"), then emit exactly one compact search phrase formatted as:
  <AlbumTitle> album artist genre
  Use only the visible album title as it appears in summary_1 followed by the three keywords: album artist genre. Do not add punctuation, explanatory words, alternate tokens, or candidate artist names.
- Do not apply this special-case if summary_1 already contains the artist or genre, or if the album anchor is an ambiguous short token that requires human clarification.

Fallback and downstream behavior:
- If retrieval using the emitted query returns no useful passages, the downstream agent may then ask the user for clarification or additional context. Do not ask the user inside the `query` field.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Task: Integrate the first-hop `context` with the newly retrieved `passages` and produce a single concise `summary` that preserves the facts needed by the final answer. Resolve the earlier bridge when the supplied passages support doing so; retain uncertainty only when the post-retrieval evidence is genuinely insufficient, ambiguous, or conflicting.

Guidelines:
- Treat a concise answer in `context` as the primary claim. Do not replace that concise context answer unless the `passages` contain a direct, identity-aligned contradiction.
- Before attributing any fact from a passage to the entity or relation named in `context`, confirm identity alignment (clear aliasing or explicit linking). Do not assume two similarly named mentions are the same without such alignment.
- If the `passages` consistently corroborate the context claim and are identity-aligned, append one brief paraphrased supporting sentence that cites the corroboration (keep the summary compact).
- If the `passages` are weak, ambiguous, or conflict with each other or with the context claim (but do not provide an explicit, identity-aligned contradiction), preserve the context claim and append a single short clause that flags the uncertainty or contradiction. When flagging, you may optionally include a verbatim quoted phrase from a passage of up to 10 words to show the source wording. Do not invent resolutions beyond what the `passages` show.
- If the `passages` contain an explicit, identity-aligned contradiction to the context claim, state that the passages contradict the context claim and briefly note the conflicting passage fact (optionally with a ≤10-word quote). Only in this case may the summary stop asserting the context claim as definitive.
- Keep the `summary` compact (aim for 1–3 sentences). Use uncertainty language only when a required premise, identity link, or relation is genuinely missing, ambiguous, or conflicting. Do not default to uncertainty when composition across context and passages licenses a conclusion.
- You may combine multiple explicit, identity-aligned facts from `context` and `passages` to form a licensed multi-hop conclusion; the supporting facts need not all appear in a single passage.
- Do not include long verbatim quotes, do not invent facts, and do not attempt to resolve contradictions beyond reporting them as present in the `passages`.

Output: a single `summary` string that follows the rules above.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the field `answer`.

Use only the information in the provided summaries as evidence. summary_2 is the later, integrated evidence state and should be the primary source for deciding the minimal answer; summary_1 may contain useful first-hop compression or retrieval-oriented language and may be used to supply premises that, together with summary_2, license a conclusion. Do not invent facts or use outside knowledge.

Emit only the minimal answer string supported by the visible summaries in the `answer` field. Do not include explanations, justification, or any additional text.

Preserve multi-hop composition: you may combine multiple explicit, identity-aligned facts across the summaries to form a licensed conclusion even when no single sentence states the whole relation. Distinguish licensed composition from unsupported inference; emit uncertainty only when a required premise, identity link, or relation is genuinely missing, ambiguous, or conflicting in the summaries.

Minimal-answer formatting rule (explicit):
- If the summaries jointly support one minimal literal and that literal appears verbatim in the summaries and consists of multiple contiguous tokens (for example: a full personal name, a full date, or a place name), emit that full literal exactly as it appears in the summaries (verbatim, unchanged). Do not truncate or coerce it to a single token.
- If the jointly supported minimal answer is itself a single-token label, emit exactly that single token (in lowercase) and nothing else.

When in doubt about whether the summaries license a single definitive literal, prefer to emit uncertainty only if the required supporting premise or identity link is actually missing, ambiguous, or conflicting. Do not make uncertainty the default when composition or transitive reasoning is required and the necessary premises are present.

Ensure the `answer` field contains only the minimal answer string supported by the summaries (subject to the formatting rule above).

response.hop1_query: The musician also known as "Braddah Iz"  is a member of what Hawaiian musical group?
hop1_docs: Israel Kamakawiwoʻole | Israel Kaʻanoʻi Kamakawiwoʻole (] , translation: "The Fearless Eyed"; May 20, 1959 – June 26, 1997), also called "Braddah Iz" (Brother Iz), was a Native Hawaiian musician, entertainer and Hawaiian sovereignty activist.

Alone in IZ World | Alone in IZ World is an album by the Hawaiian musician Israel Kamakawiwo'ole released in 2001, 4 years after his death in 1997.  The album has charted on several of Billboard’s album charts.  These are:

Gosti iz budushchego | Gosti iz budushchego (Russian: Гости из будущего , lit.: "Guests from the Future") was a Russian dance-pop group, which existed since 1996 until 2009.  It was formed by the singer Eva Polna and musician Yuri Usachyov.  The band reached high positions in Russian music charts with several songs and albums.

Kuana Torres Kahele | Kuana Torres Kahele (born September 7, 1978) is a Hawaiian musician, vocalist, songwriter, record producer, dancer, and educator from Hilo, Hawaii.  Known primarily for his original acoustic compositions, Kahele arranges traditional Hawaiian music as well, performing and recording with instruments such as ukulele, guitar, ipu, and bass.  The majority of his lyrics are written and performed in the Hawaiian language.  After learning kane dancing and mele writing in his teens, in 1995 Kahele co-founded Nā Palapalai, a Hawaiian music group that has released seven albums.  Several have charted in the top five on the "Billboard" Top World Albums chart, and the group has won a large number of Nā Hōkū Hanohano Awards, including Group of the Year.
N Dis Life | In Dis Life is the final album of original music by Native Hawaiian singer Israel "Iz" Kamakawiwo'ole before his death, released in 1996.  It reached #3 at Top World Music Albums.

Dennis Pavao | Dennis Pavao (July 11, 1951 - January 19, 2002), was one of several Hawaiian musicians who, during the 1970s, led a Hawaiian music renaissance, reviving Hawaiian music, especially "ka leo ki'eki'e," or Hawaiian falsetto singing.  Along with his cousins, Ledward and Nedward Kaʻ apana, Pavao started the group Hui ʻ Ohana.  Hui ʻ Ohana became the premier falsetto group in Hawaiʻ i.  After the breakup of Hui ʻ Ohana, Dennis Pavao moved on to pursue a solo career.

Cold Case Files (album) | Cold Case Files is a compilation album released by rap group, Onyx.  It was released on August 19, 2008 through the Iceman Music Group and contained unreleased tracks from the group's first three albums, Bacdafucup, All We Got Iz Us and Shut 'Em Down.  Deceased member, Big DS and affiliate, X-1 also appear on the album.
response.summary_1: Passage [1] identifies the musician as "Israel Kamakawiwoʻole" and that he was "also called 'Braddah Iz' (Brother Iz)" and gives his lifespan as May 20, 1959 and June 26, 1997. None of the provided passages state the name of the Hawaiian musical group he was a member of. Next retrieval should target Israel Kamakawiwoʻole's biography/career (e.g., search for "Israel Kamakawiwoʻole member of" or his full Wikipedia/biography page) to find the specific group name.
hop2_docs: Mākaha Sons | The Mākaha Sons of Ni'ihau are a Hawaiian musical group formed in 1976 on the island of Oahu by Jerome Koko, Louis "Moon" Kauakahi, Skippy Kamakawiwo'ole, Israel Kamakawiwoʻole, and Sam Gray.  The band started its career as an opening act at a small nightclub called Uptown Yokos.  The group has reorganized several times, with some members seeking other professional careers.  After the deaths of the Kamakawiwoʻole brothers Skippy and Israel, the remaining members dropped the "of Niihau" and became The Makaha Sons.

Israel Kamakawiwoʻole | Israel Kaʻanoʻi Kamakawiwoʻole (] , translation: "The Fearless Eyed"; May 20, 1959 – June 26, 1997), also called "Braddah Iz" (Brother Iz), was a Native Hawaiian musician, entertainer and Hawaiian sovereignty activist.
Jon de Mello | Jon de Mello born in Honolulu and raised in Waikiki is an American record producer, manager, composer, artist, very famous for producing Israel "IZ" Kamakawiwoʻole.

Somewhere Over the Rainbow/What a Wonderful World | "Somewhere Over the Rainbow/What a Wonderful World" is a medley of the songs "Over the Rainbow" and "What a Wonderful World" by Israel Kamakawiwoʻole and released on his albums "Ka ʻAnoʻi" and "Facing Future".

Facing Future | Facing Future is an album by Hawaiian singer Israel Kamakawiwoʻole, released in 1993.  The best-selling album of all time by a Hawaiian artist, "Facing Future" combines traditional Hawaiian-language songs, hapa haole songs with traditional instrumentation and two Jawaiian (Island reggae) tracks.  The album's sales have been driven not only by its popularity with Hawaiian audiences but by its popularity in the mainland United States and around the world, particularly the track "Somewhere Over the Rainbow/What a Wonderful World," which has been licensed for use in movies ("Finding Forrester", "Meet Joe Black", "50 First Dates", "Fred Claus", "Hubble", "Son of the Mask") television shows ("ER", "Cold Case", "Jon & Kate Plus 8", "Life On Mars", "Glee") and commercials (eToys).  "Facing Future" reached platinum status in 2005.

Israel Cricket Association | Israel Cricket Association is the official governing body of the sport of cricket in Israel.  Israel Cricket Association is Israel's representative at the International Cricket Council and is an associate member and has been a member of that body since 1974.  Its principal object is to incorporate and manage cricket within.  Israel along with Denmark, Gibraltar, Holland, Ireland, Italy and Scotland founded the European Cricket Council in 1996.

Israel–Spain relations | Israel–Spain relations refers to diplomatic, cultural and economic relations between Israel and Spain.  Israel and Spain have maintained diplomatic ties since 1986.  Israel has an embassy in Madrid.  Spain has an embassy in Tel Aviv, an honorary consulate in Haifa and a General Consulate in Jerusalem, which is regarded as diplomatic missions to the city of Jerusalem (including both West and East Jerusalem), Gaza and the territories of the West Bank.  In additions to both countries being member states of the United Nations, both countries are members of the Union for the Mediterranean.  The two countries are also involved with various programmes and agreements through the European Union, of which Spain is a member, and its relations with Israel.
hop2_titles: Somewhere Over the Rainbow/What a Wonderful World
response.summary_2: Israel Kamakawiwoʻole ("Braddah Iz") was a member and founding member of the Hawaiian group The Mākaha Sons of Niʻihau (later known simply as The Makaha Sons).
